33	# Effort Breaker
34	
35	You take **one high-effort blueprint declaration** and split it into a `\uses{}`-linked chain of **smaller, easier sub-lemmas**, so the prover formalizes a sequence of small goals instead of one large one. The target's proof becomes "by `L1`, `L2`, …, `L_n`", each `L_i` is a new block with its own statement, informal proof, and `\uses{}`, and each `L_i` is strictly easier than the original.
36	
37	You decompose DOWN (one node → many smaller ones). This is the complement of the dag-walker (which fills the cone UP). You do not invent new mathematics — you re-express the target's existing proof as a chain of named intermediate results.

67	## How you break
68	
69	1. Read the target block on disk and inspect it: `archon dag-query node --node <target> --json` (effort, deps, used-by). `archon` is on PATH; `--json` is parseable (banner on stderr).
70	2. Read the target's informal proof (and the source proof it cites, per the directive's references). Identify the **steps** — each self-contained claim the proof establishes on the way to the conclusion.
71	3. For each step, author a new sub-lemma block in the appropriate chapter:
72	   - a `\label{lem:<descriptive>}` and a `\lean{...}` hint (the Lean name the prover will use; name it by convention if the directive doesn't give one, and note it for the plan agent),
73	   - the **statement** of that step in the project's notation,
74	   - an informal `\begin{proof}` for the step with `\uses{...}` naming its own dependencies (existing blocks, Mathlib anchors, or earlier sub-lemmas in this chain),
75	   - citation discipline (below) when the step derives from a source.
76	4. **Rewrite the target's proof** to invoke the chain: a short proof body that combines `L1 … L_n`, with `\uses{lem:L1, …, lem:L_n}` (plus any direct deps that remain). The target keeps its statement and `\lean{}` unchanged.
77	5. Each `L_i` must be **strictly smaller** than the target (smaller proof, fewer moving parts). If a step is still large, either break it into sub-steps now (at fine granularity) or flag it in the report so the dispatcher can re-break it next round.

79	Granularity is the directive's call. "One level" = the proof's main steps. "Fine" = one mathematical assertion per lemma — the limit is the same as the fine-grained prover: a lemma so small its proof is a single move. There is no floor; if a break didn't help, the next dispatch breaks further.

83	- **Never add `\leanok`** — it is the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase's job.
84	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor** — when a step is a result Mathlib already provides, write it as an anchor (`\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}`, `\mathlibok`, `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}`, no proof) rather than re-proving it. Mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote — a wrong anchor is worse than leaving the step to prove.
85	- **Citation discipline (hard rule):** every block derived from a source needs `% SOURCE:` (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a verbatim original-language `% SOURCE QUOTE:` copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` first line. Never write these from memory; if you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait, read the new file, then write the cited block. Never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
86	
87	## Rules
88	
89	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). No `.lean`, no `content.tex`, no `macros/`. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
90	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces).
91	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no project-history narrative.
92	- **Preserve the target's statement and `\lean{}`.** You re-express its *proof* as a chain; you do not weaken or restate the theorem. (If the target's statement looks wrong, do NOT silently fix it — flag it under "Notes" as a strategy item.)
93	- **Conserve the mathematics.** The chain must actually prove the target — every gap the original proof crossed is covered by some `L_i`. Don't drop a hard step by relabelling it; a step you cannot decompose or prove is reported, not hidden.
94	- **Verify with the graph.** After breaking, run `archon dag-query node --node <target>` and `archon dag-query ancestors --node <target>`: the target's effort should drop, the new `L_i` should appear, and there must be no broken `\uses{}`. Fix and re-check.

102	5. For any step needing a source you lack, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for quasi-coherent sheaf morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	
96	
97	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
98	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
99	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
100	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
101	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
102	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
103	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
104	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
105	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
106	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
107	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
108	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
109	\end{proof}
110	
111	\begin{lemma}\leanok
112	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
113	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
115	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
116	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
117	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
118	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
119	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
120	  isomorphism.
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
125	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
126	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
127	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
128	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
129	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
130	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
131	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
132	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
133	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
134	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
135	\end{proof}
136	
137	\begin{lemma}\leanok
138	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
139	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
141	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
142	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
143	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
144	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
145	\end{lemma}
146	
147	\begin{proof}
148	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
149	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
150	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
151	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
152	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
153	  affine opens.
154	\end{proof}
155	
156	\subsection{The affine computation}
157	
158	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
159	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
160	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
161	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
162	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
163	
164	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
165	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
166	their proof sketches.
167	
168	\begin{lemma}
169	
170	\leanok
171	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
172	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
173	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
174	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
175	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
176	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
177	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
178	  \[
179	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
180	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
181	  \]
182	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
183	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
184	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
185	\end{lemma}
186	
187	\begin{proof}
188	
189	
190	
191	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
192	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
193	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
194	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
195	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
196	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
197	\end{proof}
198	
199	\begin{lemma}
200	
201	\leanok
202	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
203	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
205	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
206	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
207	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
208	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
209	  \[
210	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
211	    \;\cong\;
212	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
213	  \]
214	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
215	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
216	  of \(N\).
217	\end{lemma}
218	
219	\begin{proof}
220	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
221	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
222	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
223	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
224	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
225	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf

471	\begin{lemma}\leanok
472	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
473	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}

480	% scalar-tower instances feed lem:powers_restrictScalars; lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule
481	% supplies the R'-side localization). The single remaining obligation is the open-naturality of
482	% the section comparison e_{D(a)}, established as a prose remark in the proof of
483	% lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt; the proof sketch below derives each per-a hloc(a) from the top-level

490	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
491	    \;\cong\;
492	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
493	  \]

497	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
498	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
499	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to

502	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
503	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
504	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
505	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
506	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
507	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
508	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.

510	
511	\begin{proof}
512	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars, lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
513	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
514	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
515	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
516	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
517	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
518	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
519	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
520	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
521	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
522	
523	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
524	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
525	  \[
526	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
527	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
528	  \]
529	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
530	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
531	
532	  \medskip
533	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
534	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
535	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
536	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
537	
538	  \medskip
539	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
540	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
541	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
542	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
543	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
544	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
545	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
546	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
547	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
548	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
549	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
550	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
551	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
552	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
553	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
554	  an isomorphism.

557	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
558	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
559	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
560	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
561	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
562	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
563	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
564	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
565	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
566	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
567	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
568	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
569	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
570	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
571	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
572	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
573	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
574	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
575	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
576	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
577	  (the naturality remark in the proof of
578	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}). This proceeds in three
579	  movements.
580	
581	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
582	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
583	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
584	  \[
585	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
586	      \;\cong\;
587	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
588	  \]

590	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
591	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
592	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
593	
594	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
595	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism

631	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
632	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
633	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
634	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
635	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property
636	  across that square — via the transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized
637	  modules — yields that \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) is the
638	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
639	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)
640	  is performed: the only \(a\)-dependent input is the instantiation of the single
641	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
642	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
643	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
644	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
645	
646	  \medskip
647	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
648	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
649	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
650	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
651	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the naturality
652	  remark in the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the family
653	  \(\{e_U\}_U\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the
654	  structure-sheaf restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this
655	  open-naturality that drives the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the
656	  per-\(a\) localization fact \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level
657	  localization Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with that
658	  naturality square for the inclusion
659	  \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a section-level naturality square
660	  re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\). This is the single remaining
661	  obligation of the construction; the carrier of the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
662	  sections is the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\), against which
663	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} applies directly.
664	
665	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
666	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
667	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
668	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
669	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
670	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
671	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
672	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
673	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).

676	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
677	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
678	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
679	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.

682	  \medskip
683	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
684	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
685	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
686	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
687	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
688	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
689	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
690	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
691	  needed for the affine lane.
692	\end{proof}

694	\begin{remark}
695	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
696	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
697	  \begin{enumerate}
698	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
699	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
700	      \(R\)-module of sections of
701	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
702	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
703	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
704	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
705	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
706	      closed under isomorphism; hence
707	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
708	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
709	      needed for the affine lane.
710	  \end{enumerate}
711	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
712	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
713	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
714	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
715	\end{remark}

% --- The three one-cancellation atoms of the eCancel telescoping (iter-024 fbc-ecancel). ---
% Each is an independently-formalizable lemma whose proof is essentially a single move; together
% with the proved unit-distribution engine `lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp` they telescope the
% inner composite against the codomain read down to the lone affine `(Spec ιA)`-unit. Signatures
% below were drafted against, and elaboration-checked at, the live goal state of

\begin{lemma}
[(A-2a) The $e$-unit is an isomorphism]
  \label{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit}
  % LEAN SIGNATURE:
  %   theorem base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (e : X ⟶ Y) [IsIso e]
  %       (N : Y.Modules) :
  %       IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction e).unit.app N)
  % (Proof: `haveI := pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso e; infer_instance` — the unit of an
  %  adjunction whose left adjoint `pullback e` is an equivalence is an isomorphism.)
  \uses{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
  Let \(e : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes and \(N\) a \(Y\)-module. Then the unit
  \(\eta^{e}_{N} : N \to e_{*}\,e^{*}N\) of the \((e^{*} \dashv e_{*})\)-adjunction is an
  isomorphism. This is the single fact enabling cancellation~(1): in the inner telescoping it is
  the \(e\)-unit factor \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}\eta^{e}\) (here \(e\) is the canonical
  square isomorphism \(\mathrm{pullbackSpecIso}\)), which the codomain read
  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) bakes in as its inverse \(\eta^{e}\)-piece, so the two cancel.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
  Since \(e\) is an isomorphism of schemes, its pullback functor \(e^{*} = \mathrm{pullback}\,e\)
  is an equivalence (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}). The unit of an adjunction whose
  left adjoint is an equivalence is an isomorphism; hence \(\eta^{e}_{N}\) is invertible. A single
  instance-resolution step.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[(A-2b) The surviving $\mathrm{pushforwardComp}$ factor has identity $\Gamma$-image]
  \label{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp}
  % LEAN SIGNATURE:
  %   theorem base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp {X₁ X₂ : Scheme.{u}}

  %       (moduleSpecΓFunctor (R := R)).map
  %           ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward (Spec.map φ)).map
  %             ((Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp a b).hom.app M))
  %         = 𝟙 _
  % (Proof: `(pushforwardComp a b).hom.app M = 𝟙` by rfl; then `(Spec φ)_*.map_id` and
  %  `moduleSpecΓFunctor.map_id`. This is the `(Spec φ)_*`-lifted form of the existing helper
  %  `gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id`, which covers the unwrapped factor `b = Spec φ` directly.)
  \uses{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}
  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : X_1 \to X_2\), \(b : X_2 \to \operatorname{Spec} A\), a ring
  map \(\varphi : R \to A\), and a module \(M\) on \(X_1\), the \(\Gamma\)-image over
  \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) of the \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_{*}\)-image of the
  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b}\) hom-coherence is the identity. This discharges
  cancellation~(2): the \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}\) factor surviving
  the unit distribution drops out under \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_{*}(-)\bigr)\),
  exactly as the unwrapped \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',\operatorname{Spec}\varphi}\) factor drops
  by Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}
  The section value of the \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}\) hom-coherence is the identity at every open,
  so \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) is the identity morphism; its
  \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_{*}\)-image is then the identity by functoriality, and its further
  \(\Gamma\)-image is the identity by functoriality again. Two applications of the functorial
  identity law.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[(A-2c) The $\mathrm{pullbackComp}$ factor cancels its inverse in the codomain read]
  \label{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pullbackComp}
